name: Phenolic Resin Composites Laser Cleaning Dataset
description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for Phenolic Resin Composites. Includes thermal, optical, mechanical, and laser interaction properties validated against industry standards.

license.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
license.description: Free to share and adapt with attribution

measurementTechnique: Laser ablation testing, material characterization, spectroscopy

includedInDataCatalog.name: Z-Beam Material Properties Database
includedInDataCatalog.description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for industrial applications

powerRange.description: Optimal average power for phenolic resin composite cleaning without thermal damage

wavelength.description: Near-IR wavelength optimized for phenolic resin absorption and minimal substrate damage

spotSize.description: Beam spot diameter for precise cleaning control on composite surfaces

repetitionRate.description: Optimal repetition rate for efficient cleaning with thermal management

energyDensity.description: Fluence threshold for effective phenolic resin removal

pulseWidth.description: Nanosecond pulse duration for controlled ablation of phenolic resin matrix

scanSpeed.description: Optimal scanning speed for uniform cleaning coverage

passCount.description: Recommended number of passes for complete contaminant removal

overlapRatio.description: Optimal beam overlap for uniform cleaning without excessive heat accumulation

power.description: Typical range for pulsed Nd:YAG lasers in composite cleaning to avoid thermal damage; starting at 100W for light contamination on phenolics, which have low thermal conductivity (~0.2 W/m·K). Max safe ~300W to prevent charring (melting point ~200-300°C). Source: IPG Photonics specs and studies in Optics and Lasers in Engineering (2018) on polymer composites.

scan_speed.description: Effective range for 1064 nm cleaning of contaminants; slower (500-1000 mm/s) for heavy soiling, faster (2000-3000 mm/s) for light; 1500 mm/s optimal for uniform removal without substrate heating on low-reflectivity phenolics (~10-20% at 1064 nm). Source: Industry standards from Laserax manuals and J. Cleaner Production (2020) case studies.

pulse_width.description: Nanosecond regime preferred over ps/fs for cost-effective cleaning with minimal heat-affected zone (HAZ <50 μm); 20 ns balances ablation efficiency and reduced thermal diffusion in phenolics. Ps (10-100 ps) for ultra-precise work but higher cost. Source: Laser physics in Applied Surface Science (2019) on polymer ablation thresholds.

repetition_rate.description: Typical for industrial cleaning; 30 kHz recommended for phenolics to achieve fast processing while limiting heat accumulation (high freq >50 kHz risks cumulative heating due to low conductivity). Trade-off: higher rates speed up cleaning but increase HAZ. Source: Academic literature in Journal of Laser Applications (2021).

energy_density.description: Fluence range for contaminant removal; damage threshold for phenolics ~1.5-2.5 J/cm² at 1064 nm due to moderate absorption; 1.0 J/cm² safe for cleaning without ablation of substrate. Conservative for thermal sensitivity. Source: Ablation studies in Composites Part B (2017) and Optics Express (2022).

spot_size.description: Beam diameter range for precision vs. area coverage; 300 μm recommended for phenolic composites in surface prep to focus energy and minimize overshoot on irregular surfaces. Smaller for fine details, larger for broad areas. Source: Equipment specs from Coherent Inc. and process optimization in Industrial Laser Solutions (2020).

pass_count.description: Single pass for light contamination, multi-pass (2-3) for heavy; 2 passes typical for complete removal on phenolics without excessive heat buildup. Source: Industry practice in aerospace case studies (e.g., AIAA Journal, 2019) and manufacturer guidelines.

overlap_ratio.description: Ensures uniform coverage; 50% recommended for phenolic composites to avoid gaps or overexposure, balancing efficiency and even fluence distribution. Higher (60-80%) for heavy contamination. Source: Process optimization in Surface and Coatings Technology (2021).
